**Code under review** ```javascript function renderHTML(userInput) { document.getElementById('output').innerHTML = userInput; } ``` --- ## 1. Security Issues | Issue | Why it’s a problem | How to fix it | |-------|-------------------|---------------| | **Cross‑Site Scripting (XSS)** – direct assignment to `innerHTML` | Any HTML/JS supplied by the user is parsed and executed by the browser. An attacker can inject `` or event‑handler attributes (``) and run arbitrary code in the victim’s context. | • **Never trust raw user input** for `innerHTML`.
• If you must insert HTML, **sanitize** it with a trusted library (e.g., [DOMPurify](https://github.com/cure53/DOMPurify)).
• Prefer **text‑only insertion** (`textContent`) when the intent is to display plain text. | | **Missing Content Security Policy (CSP) mitigation** | Even if you sanitize, a CSP that disallows `unsafe-inline` scripts provides a defense‑in‑depth layer. | Deploy a strict CSP (e.g., `script-src 'self'; object-src 'none'; base-uri 'self';`) and report violations. | | **Potential injection via data‑attributes or SVG** | Some XSS vectors survive simple HTML‑escaping (e.g., `
`const el = document.getElementById('output'); if (el) { … } else { console.warn('Output element not found'); }` | | **No type checking** | Passing a non‑string (e.g., `null`, `undefined`, an object) will be coerced to a string (`"[object Object]"`) which may be unexpected. | Validate/coerce explicitly:
